title: A Genome-Scale Proteomic Screen Identifies a Role for DnaK in Chaperoning of Polar Autotransporters in Shigella
abstract: Autotransporters are outer membrane proteins that are widely distributed among gram-negative bacteria. Like other autotransporters, the Shigella autotransporter IcsA, which is required for actin assembly during infection, is secreted at the bacterial pole. In the bacterial cytoplasm, IcsA localizes to poles and potential cell division sites independent of the cell division protein FtsZ. To identify bacterial proteins involved in the targeting of IcsA to the pole in the bacterial cytoplasm, we screened a genome-scale library of E scherichia coli proteins tagged with green fluorescent protein (GFP) for those that displayed a localization pattern similar to that of IcsA-GFP in cells that lack functional FtsZ using a strain carrying a temperature-sensitive ftsZ allele. For each protein that mimicked the localization of IcsA-GFP, we tested whether IcsA localization was dependent on the presence of the protein. Although these approaches did not identify a polar receptor for IcsA, the cytoplasmic chaperone DnaK both mimicked IcsA localization at elevated temperatures as a GFP fusion and was required for the localization of IcsA to the pole in the cytoplasm of E. coli . DnaK was also required for IcsA secretion at the pole in S higella flexneri . The localization of DnaK-GFP to poles and potential cell division sites was dependent on elevated growth temperature and independent of the presence of IcsA or functional FtsZ; native DnaK was found to be enhanced at midcell and the poles. A second Shigella autotransporter, SepA, also required DnaK for secretion, consistent with a role of DnaK more generally in the chaperoning of autotransporter proteins in the bacterial cytoplasm.

MATERIALS AND METHODS.content: Bacterial strains used in this study are listed in Table 1 . E. coli strains were grown in Luria broth (LB) and maintained on LB agar plates. S. flexneri strains were grown in tryptic soy broth and maintained on tryptic soy broth agar plates that contained the dye Congo red. Antibiotics were used at the following concentrations: ampicillin, 100 μg ml −1 ; chloramphenicol, 20 μg ml −1 ; and tetracycline, 12.5 μg ml −1 . Isopropyl-β- d -thiogalactopyranoside (IPTG) was added to 1 mM and l -arabinose to 0.2% where appropriate. E. coli EC307 [ ftsZ84 (Ts)], E. coli BW25113 dnaK , and the S. flexneri dnaK depletion strain were maintained at 30°C unless otherwise stated. Plasmid DNA from strains that contain an N-terminal His 6 tag and a C-terminal gfp fused in frame to open reading frames (ORFs) in E. coli were used. The expression of E. coli ORFs was under the control of an IPTG-inducible P T5/ lac promoter of pCA24N ( 38 ).

Plasmids pBAD24-IcsA 507-620 -GFP, pBAD24-IcsA 1-104 -GFP, and pBAD33-IcsA 507-620 -GFP, which direct the synthesis of hybrid proteins consisting of a segment of IcsA fused to GFP under the control of the arabinose promoter of pBAD24 or pBAD33, and pBAD24-GFP, which directs the synthesis of GFP alone, have been described ( 13 , 51 ). icsA 1 - 104 - gfp and gfp were subcloned as MluI-HindIII fragments from pBAD24- icsA 1 - 1 04 :: gfp and pBAD24- gfp into pBAD33 to generate pBAD33- icsA 1 - 1 04 :: gfp and pBAD33- gfp . To generate a constitutive complementing plasmid containing dnaK and dnaJ as a single transcriptional unit, a 3,870-bp fragment containing both genes and the Shine-Dalgarno sequence upstream of dnaK were amplified by PCR. The PCR product was digested with NsiI and SmaI and cloned into the PstI and SspI sites of pBR322. To generate an inducible complementing plasmid containing dnaK and dnaJ as a single transcriptional unit, first dnaK was cloned as an MluI-SalI fragment into pBAD33. Then, a 1,310-bp fragment containing the extreme 3′ end of the dnaK coding sequence and the complete dnaJ coding sequence was amplified by PCR and cloned as a SalI fragment into SalI-digested pBAD33- dnaK . To generate pBAD24-mCherry, the coding sequence for a derivative of mCherry that had been codon optimized for E. coli (gift of K. Marians and S. Sandler) was amplified by PCR and the PCR product cloned as an XbaI-SphI fragment into pBAD24. To then generate pBAD24-IcsA 507-620 -mCherry, a fragment containing the coding sequence for IcsA residues 507 to 620 was amplified by PCR and cloned upstream of and in frame with the coding sequence for mCherry. Sequence analysis was performed to verify that each construct was correct.

Chromosomal deletions in genes of interest were obtained from the Genobase consortium ( 3 ). Briefly, mutations were created using the λ Red recombination method, essentially as described previously ( 16 ). Seventy-nucleotide (nt) primers were used with 50-nt homology to the upstream or downstream flanking regions to the gene of interest on the 5′ end of the primer and 20-nt homology to the FLP recombination target (FRT)-flanked kanamycin (Kan) cassette at the 3′ end as described previously ( 3 ). Isogenic strains were constructed by the P1-mediated transduction of each mutation into a strain carrying the plasmid bearing the icsA :: gfp fusions of interest.

Given that DnaK was encoded by an essential gene ( 23 , 78 ), a conditional null mutant was generated. dnaKJ was cloned under the control of an arabinose-dependent promoter in pBAD33 ( 27 ) or under the control of the native promoter onto pBR322, and the plasmid was introduced into strain BW25113 or 2457T. The native chromosomal copy of the gene was then disrupted by the P1 transduction of the Kan-resistant allele from strain BW25113 Δ dnaK ::FRT- kan -FRT into each of these strains at 30°C, at which temperature dnaK is not essential ( 2 , 11 , 21 , 23 , 77 , 79 ). Plasmids bearing the icsA :: gfp fusions of interest were transformed into each strain.

EC307 [ ftsZ84 (Ts)] cells were made chemically competent using CaCl 2 , and 30 μl of competent cells was transferred into each well of sterile 96-well plates, exclusive of wells at the edges of the plate, which were not used due to the increased evaporation that occurs from them. Two microliters of DNA from each plasmid was transformed by heat shock into the competent cells contained in the 96-well plates, 120 μl of rich medium was added to enable recovery at 30°C, and the recovered samples were plated for single colonies on LB with chloramphenicol at 30°C. Four transformants were reisolated on appropriate medium, and one colony from each was inoculated into 100 μl of fresh LB with chloramphenicol in a 96-well format at 30°C. To overnight cultures, 100 μl of fresh medium containing 30% glycerol was added, and the strains were stored frozen in the 96-well plates. Each 96-well plate also included a well containing EC307 carrying each of the two plasmids bearing icsA :: gfp controls. Individual GFP clones were inoculated from the frozen stocks into wells of a sterile 96-well plate containing 100 μl of LB medium with chloramphenicol or ampicillin (for plasmids bearing icsA :: gfp ) at 30°C. Overnight cultures were subcultured 1:50 into appropriate medium in a sterile 96-well plate and grown for an hour at 30°C on a platform shaker (Lab-Line Instruments, Inc.) at approximately 500 rpm. The 96-well plate was then transferred to a platform shaker at the nonpermissive temperature (42°C) and grown for another hour. The synthesis of GFP-tagged IcsA constructs were induced by the addition of l -arabinose for 10 min at 42°C. Subsequently, bacterial cultures were subcultured 1:5 into glass-bottomed 96-well plates containing fresh medium at 42°C and centrifuged at 2,000 rpm for 10 min at 42°C to bring the bacteria into contact with the glass bottom of the wells. The cells were visualized in the 96-well glass-bottomed plates with the stage incubator set at 42°C using a 100× oil immersion objective on a Nikon TE300 microscope with Chroma Technology filters. For approximately 590 of the 725 experimental clones, the GFP signal was visualized without the induction of the GFP-tagged protein. For the remaining approximately 135 clones, IPTG was added to 1 mM for 10 min at 42°C prior to visualization to induce the synthesis of the GFP-tagged protein.

The follow-up screening of individual GFP-tagged proteins, encoded on pCA24N, that displayed the appropriate GFP localization phenotypes was performed by examining strain EC307 carrying these proteins individually on glass slides, as described previously ( 36 ). Briefly, cells were grown to an optical density at 600 nm (OD 600 ) of 0.3 to 0.4 at the permissive temperature (30°C), at which time one half of the culture was shifted to the restrictive temperature (42°C) and the other half maintained at the permissive temperature for an additional 60 min. At this point, those GFP-tagged proteins that required induction to be visualized were induced with IPTG for an additional 10 min at 42°C or for 30 min at 30°C. For icsA :: gfp or icsA ::mCherry constructs or gfp or mCherry controls, similar growth conditions were used and expression was induced by the addition of l -arabinose to 0.2% for 20 min at 37°C or for 60 min at 30°C. Where used for the filamentation of cells, aztreonam was added to 1 μg/ml prior to the induction of protein synthesis, and incubation with aztreonam was continued for 60 to 90 min. The cells were either mounted onto a 1% agarose pad on a 15-well slide (ICN) and visualized live as described previously ( 13 ) or were fixed in 3.7% paraformaldehyde in phosphate-buffered saline and mounted on cover glasses. The imaging of GFP-tagged DnaK was performed using pCA24N- dnaK . The leakiness of the promoter permitted the sufficient synthesis of GFP-tagged DnaK to allow visualization; the expression of the fusion was not induced. The immunofluorescence of DnaK was performed by fixing and permeabilizing cells as previously described ( 36 ), followed by labeling with a 1:100 dilution of monoclonal antibody to DnaK (Stressgen, Ann Arbor, MI) and a Texas Red anti-mouse secondary antibody.

Overnight cultures were diluted 1:100 and grown for 1 h and 30 min at 37°C in medium containing 0.1% arabinose (DnaK replete conditions) to an OD 600 of 0.3 to 0.4. Bacteria were recovered by centrifugation, washed, further diluted to an OD 600 of 0.005, and grown with (replete) or without (deplete) arabinose for 4 h at 37°C to an OD 600 of 0.8 to 2.0. The samples were spun and washed with fresh medium to remove IcsA secreted during the period of DnaK depletion. Cultures were then diluted to 0.2 and grown for 1 h and 30 min at 37°C, at which time the preparation of bacteria for immunolabeling or the preparation of protein samples was performed.

Labeling of secreted IcsA on the surface of intact bacteria was performed as previously described ( 24 ). Western blot analysis was performed using standard protocols. Proteins were prepared from cell pellets and culture supernatants as described previously ( 74 ). The amount of supernatant protein loaded per lane corresponded to protein derived from sixfold more culture than the amount of whole-cell protein loaded per lane. Rabbit antibodies to IcsA and SepA have been described ( 35 ). Monoclonal antibody to DnaK was from Stressgen (Ann Arbor, MI) and rabbit antibody to isocitrate dehydrogenase was a gift from A. L. Sonenshein.

Bioinformatic analysis to determine gene conservation and predicted membrane topology was performed using the following open source sites: Swiss-Prot ( http://kr.expasy.org/sprot/ ), Microbial Genome Workbench ( http://www.bioscinet.org/cgi-bin/mgw.pl ), and NCBI BLAST ( http://blast.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/Blast.cgi ).

Microscopy was performed using a 100× oil immersion objective on a Nikon TE300 with Chroma Technology filters. Images were captured digitally using a black-and-white Photometrics CoolSnap HQ or Nikon DigiSight Monochrome charge-coupled-device camera and IP Laboratory (Scanalytics) or Nikon Elements Basic software. Images were assembled using Adobe Photoshop software, and color images were pseudocolored using the same software. To determine the significance of differences among data sets, standard statistical methods were used.

RESULTS.content: The localization of numerous bacterial proteins has been studied using GFP tags. To facilitate genome-wide analyses of protein localization in E. coli , Kitagawa et al. generated a complete set of E. coli ORFs, each translationally fused to gfp ( 38 ). Each ORF was cloned into the high-copy vector pCA24N, in frame at its 5′ end with sequences encoding a six-histidine tag, followed by a seven- amino-acid spacer, and at its 3′ end with sequences encoding a five-amino-acid spacer, followed by gfp ( 38 ). The expression of the gene encoding each tagged protein is under the control of the T5/ lac promoter and is repressed by the expression of a plasmid-borne lacI q ( 38 ). Genome coverage was greater than 99.7%, as all but nine of the predicted ORFs were successfully cloned into the expression vector ( 38 ). An analysis of the distribution of each GFP-tagged ORF protein was performed, and the distribution of the signal was categorized as diffuse, membrane associated, present in foci, membrane associated as well as in foci, present in rings, nucleoid associated, nucleoid associated plus foci, or undefined ( http://ecoli.aist-nara.ac.jp/GFP/gfp_top.jsp ). Proteins that localize to cell poles display distributions that are generally characterized as foci present at the pole ( 8 , 13 , 28 , 66 ). In the initial classification of protein distribution described above, whether foci were localized to cell poles was not determined. Therefore, the input into our screen included all 826 ORFs that had been classified as displaying foci, either alone or in combination with another distribution (697 ORFs that were present in foci, 99 ORFs that were membrane associated as well as in foci, and 30 ORFs that were nucleoid associated plus foci), as well as the 8 ORFs that displayed rings, since rings are often associated with the cell division site. The majority of the proteins encoded by these 834 ORFs are predicted to be cytoplasmic. The proper distribution of cytoplasmic proteins will only occasionally be disrupted by the fusion of a GFP tag to the protein's carboxyl terminus. However, since the Sec apparatus is unable to translocate folded proteins the size of GFP, a GFP tag at the carboxyl terminus of a protein normally secreted via the Sec apparatus might interfere with the protein's proper distribution. Therefore, we eliminated from consideration the 109 ORFs encoding proteins whose carboxyl termini are known or predicted to be extracytoplasmic. Integral inner membrane proteins whose carboxyl termini are known or predicted to remain on the cytoplasmic side of the inner membrane were maintained in the input pool. Also not excluded were four ORFs that encode proteins secreted by the twin arginine translocation pathway (AmiC, FhuD, TorZ, and YcdB) ( 7 , 33 , 68 ), since folded GFP is efficiently translocated through the twin arginine translocation apparatus ( 71 ). Thus, the final input pool consisted of 725 ORFs.

In the absence of functional FtsZ, E. coli cells grow as filaments because FtsZ is required for septation. In these filaments, a derivative of IcsA that consists of a minimal sequence capable of localizing to the pole and that remains in the cytoplasm because the signal peptide has been removed (IcsA 507-620 -GFP) localizes to the cell poles and to each potential cell division site along the length of the filament (Fig. 1B and C ) ( 36 ). Since we wished to identify proteins that might serve as a receptor for IcsA at these sites, we screened for those E. coli ORFs in our input pool that, in cells depleted of functional FtsZ, displayed a localization pattern similar to that of IcsA 507-620 -GFP. The plasmids carrying each of the 725 orf :: gfp constructs were introduced into an E. coli ftsZ84 (Ts) background (strain EC307), and the distribution of the GFP signal from each GFP-tagged protein was determined in the absence of functional FtsZ by fluorescence microscopy following growth at the nonpermissive temperature. The initial screening was performed in glass-bottomed 96-well plates (Fig. 1D and as described in Materials and Methods). In the initial screen, approximately 225 GFP-tagged ORFs displayed a GFP signal distribution similar to that of IcsA 507-620 -GFP following growth at the nonpermissive temperature. Each was rescreened using agarose pads on individual glass slides, following growth at both the permissive and the nonpermissive temperatures (as described in Materials and Methods).

Although icsA is present only in Shigella spp., IcsA also localizes to the pole in E. coli , Salmonella enterica serovar Typhimurium, Yersinia pseudotuberculosis , and Vibrio cholerae when heterologously expressed in these organisms ( 13 , 58 , 59 ). Among those GFP-tagged proteins that reproducibly displayed a GFP signal at the cell poles and at potential cell division sites along the length of the filaments, we eliminated from further consideration hits that were not conserved among these species or were absent in Shigella . We also eliminated from further evaluation 28 hits that were well-characterized proteins displaying functions that appeared to be unrelated to protein localization or polarity, e.g., adenylate cyclase (Cya), the double-stranded DNA exonuclease SbcC, and the recombination proteins RecA and RecN. We designated the 64 proteins that remained as priority hits (Table 2 ). Thirty-six of these 64 proteins (56%) were encoded by “y” genes and/or annotated as hypothetical proteins, with no function ascribed in the published literature. We then explored whether priority-hit proteins might be required for the polar localization of IcsA by examining the localization of IcsA 507-620 -GFP in strains in which the gene encoding the protein had been deleted in the E. coli strain background BW25113; this assessment was restricted to the 30 proteins for which gene deletions in BW25113 were available at the time of analysis. We found that a deletion of the gene encoding the cytoplasmic chaperone DnaK led to an alteration in the distribution of IcsA-GFP (Fig. 2 ). DnaK assists in protein folding in the bacterial cytoplasm in an ATP-dependent manner, regulated by its cochaperones DnaJ, which stimulates ATP hydrolysis, and GrpE, which stimulates nucleotide exchange (reviewed in references 29 , 49 , and 76 ). In 52% ± 8% of cells of the dnaK strain, the GFP-tagged IcsA derivative, expressed from pBAD33, localized to large irregularly shaped foci that were distributed around the cytoplasm and were distinctly different in shape and distribution than the round punctate polar foci of IcsA-GFP in cells that are wild type with respect to dnaK (Fig. 2A versus 1B). The appearance of the irregularly shaped foci of IcsA-GFP that formed in the absence of DnaK was suggestive of large protein aggregates. In all other deletion strains examined, IcsA-GFP localized in small foci at the bacterial poles (Table 2 ), a pattern that was indistinguishable from its localization in the parent wild-type E. coli strain BW25113 (data not shown).

To test whether the irregularly shaped foci of IcsA 507-620 -GFP observed in the dnaK strain were due to the absence of DnaK, we complemented the mutation with dnaKJ carried on pBR322; we included both dnaK and dnaJ because they are transcriptionally linked and form a complex in the bacterial cytoplasm (reviewed in references 29 , 49 , and 76 ). We did not include multicopy grpE in the complementation, since grpE overexpression can inhibit DnaK function and cell division ( 69 ). These experiments were performed at 30°C, and in all our experiments, strains lacking dnaK were maintained at 30°C. In the presence of the dnaKJ plasmid, IcsA localization to the pole and the small punctate shape of the foci were restored (polar foci were present in 71% ± 2% of cells of the complemented strain versus 6% ± 2% of the cells carrying vector only [ P = 0.0008] [Fig. 2D ]), indicating that its mislocalization in the dnaK strain was due to the absence of DnaK or a complex containing DnaK and DnaJ. Since DnaK is required for growth at temperatures above 34°C ( 2 , 11 , 21 , 23 , 77 , 79 ), it was particularly important to test whether the mislocalization of IcsA in the BW25113 Δ dnaK strain might be due to suppressor mutations that are likely present in this strain, given that it is viable at 37°C. The apparent formation of aggregates in the cytoplasm could be due to the misfolding of either the GFP component or the IcsA component of the protein fusion. GFP alone, expressed from the same plasmid under identical growth conditions, gave a diffuse signal in greater than 99% of the cells of either the dnaK strain or the complemented dnaK strain (Fig. 2C and F ), indicating that GFP was folded and soluble even in the absence of DnaK and that the irregularly shaped foci that formed upon the synthesis of IcsA 507-620 -GFP were likely due to the IcsA portion of the tagged protein. In addition to IcsA residues 507 to 620, a second region of IcsA (residues 1 to 104) is sufficient to localize a GFP tag to the pole ( 13 ), although it is unclear whether the mechanism by which the two sequences are targeted to the pole is the same. IcsA residues 1 to 104 were also dependent on DnaK for polar localization, since IcsA 1-104 -GFP localized to the pole in the presence but not the absence of DnaK (polar foci were present in 34% ± 5% of complemented cells versus 3% ± 0.4% of the cells carrying vector only [ P = 0.01] [Fig. 2B and E ]). The difference in localization patterns in the dnaK cells and the complemented dnaK cells was unlikely to be due to instability of the GFP-tagged IcsA proteins in the absence of DnaK, since the levels of each GFP-tagged protein were similar in the two backgrounds (data not shown). Thus, each of the two IcsA sequences that are sufficient for targeting to the pole is dependent on DnaK for proper delivery to the pole, suggesting the presence of some overlap in the mechanisms involved in delivering the two IcsA sequences to the pole. In addition to localizing to potential cell division sites in cells that have filamented as a result of the loss of functional FtsZ, IcsA localizes to potential cell division sites in cells that have filamented upon the inhibition of the cell division protein FtsI by aztreonam ( 36 ). We tested whether IcsA localization to these sites in the bacterial cytoplasm depended on DnaK. Following filamentation by the addition of aztreonam, IcsA 507-620 -GFP formed irregularly shaped foci that were spaced at irregular intervals along the lengths of 60% ± 11% of the filaments of the dnaK strain and which resembled those seen in the nonfilamented cells of the same strain (Fig. 2G versus A). The formation of foci that were irregular in shape and spacing was due to the absence of DnaK in these cells, since the complementation of these cells with dnaKJ rescued both the localization of IcsA 507-620 -GFP to potential cell division sites and the punctate size and rounded shape of the foci (regularly spaced foci were present in 76% ± 16% of the filaments of the complemented cells versus 3% ± 2% of the cells carrying vector only [ P = 0.02] [Fig. 2J ]). A similar dependence on DnaK for localization to potential division sites was observed for IcsA 1-104 -GFP (regularly spaced foci were present in 60% ± 11% of the complemented cells versus <1% of the cells carrying vector only [ P = 0.02] [Fig. 2H and K ]), and GFP alone was diffuse in >99% of the filaments of both the uncomplemented and DnaK-complemented cells (Fig. 2I and L ). Thus, the delivery of IcsA to the cell poles and potential cell division sites in the bacterial cytoplasm is dependent on DnaK.

DnaK is required for the secretion of IcsA in S. flexneri . To test whether DnaK was required for the secretion of native IcsA, we examined IcsA secretion in wild-type S. flexneri strain 2457T. Like other autotransporters, after secretion across the cytoplasmic membrane via the Sec apparatus ( 9 ), the IcsA carboxy-terminal domain forms a β-barrel in the outer membrane in conjunction with the translocation of the large amino-terminal passenger domain across the outer membrane to the bacterial surface (reviewed in references 17 and 30 ). IcsA is then proteolytically cleaved at the junction of the passenger and β domains by the IcsA-specific protease IcsP (SopA) ( 19 , 62 ), thereby releasing the passenger domain into the culture supernatant. Since IcsP-mediated processing occurs only after translocation to the bacterial surface ( 74 ), the processed passenger domain in the culture supernatant represents protein that had been successfully secreted to the bacterial surface, whereas full-length IcsA present in the cell pellet represents protein that either is secreted to the bacterial surface but not processed or is not secreted to the bacterial surface. We examined the role of DnaK in IcsA secretion by comparing the IcsA in the culture supernatants and cell pellets of cells lacking DnaK to that of cells in which DnaK is present (Fig. 3A to D ). Since icsA is efficiently expressed at 37°C but not at 30°C ( 18 , 48 , 55 ), and dnaK is essential at elevated temperatures ( 53 ), we generated a S. flexneri strain in which DnaK could be depleted, by placing dnaK under the control of the arabinose-inducible promoter of pBAD33 and then disrupting the chromosomal copy of dnaK (see Materials and Methods). DnaK was efficiently depleted by growth in the absence of arabinose for 4 h (Fig. 3C ). IcsA was completely absent from the supernatants of DnaK-depleted cells (Fig. 3A ), indicating that the proteolytic cleavage of IcsA at the cell surface was not occurring because the protein was either not secreted or not processed by IcsP. This was at most partially due to decreased IcsA synthesis or IcsA degradation, since the amount of IcsA in the cell pellets was only moderately reduced by DnaK depletion (Fig. 3A ). Moreover, overall protein synthesis appeared to be maintained, since the amount of the soluble cytoplasmic protein isocitrate dehydrogenase was not significantly reduced under these conditions. Since it was possible that the absence of cleaved IcsA in the culture supernatant following DnaK depletion was due to the absence of IcsP-mediated processing of IcsA at the bacterial surface rather than a defect in IcsA secretion, we examined the translocation of IcsA to the bacterial surface by immunofluorescence. We probed the surfaces of intact S. flexneri cells using an antibody that recognizes the extracellular domain of IcsA ( 73 ). Following DnaK depletion, IcsA was detectable on the bacterial surface in a punctate pattern around the entire circumference of the cells (Fig. 4C ), whereas on the wild-type strain or the DnaK depletion strain when DnaK synthesis was maintained, IcsA was present as a cap over the bacterial pole (Fig. 4A and B , arrows). As expected, IcsA was undetectable on the surface of an icsA mutant (Fig. 4D ), confirming that the antibodies specifically recognize the IcsA protein. These results indicate that in the absence of DnaK, the secretion of IcsA at the pole and/or the maintenance of IcsA in the outer membrane at the pole is defective. These findings suggest that in the absence of DnaK, IcsA is inefficiently targeted to the pole in the bacterial cytoplasm, and as a consequence, a portion of it is secreted into the outer membrane at sites around the entire bacterial periphery. Whether the IcsP-mediated processing of surface-associated IcsA is altered in the absence of DnaK is unclear.

To address whether DnaK is required more generally for the secretion of autotransporter proteins, we examined the secretion of SepA, another autotransporter that is present in S. flexneri . In wild-type cells, SepA is efficiently proteolytically processed at the bacterial surface such that the protein is detected exclusively as a 120-kDa band in the cell supernatant and is undetectable in cell pellets ( 4 , 35 ). Following the depletion of DnaK, SepA was no longer detectable in the culture supernatant (Fig. 3B ). Under these conditions, SepA was also undetectable in the cell pellets (Fig. 3B ), indicating that, in the absence of DnaK, SepA protein that was not properly secreted was likely degraded. Thus, while these results are most consistent with DnaK being required for the secretion of SepA to the periplasm, we cannot eliminate the possibility that DnaK is instead indirectly required for another step in SepA translocation and processing.

We had initially identified DnaK because, in cells depleted of functional FtsZ, it had displayed a localization pattern similar to that of IcsA 507-620 -GFP. Given that functional FtsZ had been induced to unfold or misfold by shifting growing cells to the nonpermissive temperature (42°C), we postulated that the localization of DnaK to potential cell division sites in these filaments might be due to an accumulation of unfolded or misfolded FtsZ at these sites. We tested this postulate by comparing the localization of DnaK-GFP in the ftsZ84 strain and the parent wild-type strain MC4100, each grown either at the nonpermissive temperature (42°C) or the permissive temperature (30°C) (Fig. 5 ). When grown at 42°C, DnaK-GFP (expressed from the leaky promoter of pCA24N- dnaK without induction) localized in foci along the lengths of 93% ± 2% of the ftsZ84 filaments and in foci at the poles and midcell of 92% ± 7% of the wild-type strain cells (Fig. 5C and G ), indicating that the accumulation of DnaK-GFP to these sites was not dependent on the presence of unfolded or misfolded FtsZ. When grown at 30°C, DnaK-GFP displayed a diffuse signal in each of the strains (97% ± 2% and 95% ± 7% of cells, respectively [Fig. 5A and E ]), together indicating that elevated temperature per se induced the accumulation of DnaK-GFP in foci at these sites. In wild-type cells grown at 37°C, the localization of DnaK-GFP was similar to that in cells grown at 42°C (data not shown). We also tested whether filamentation independent of FtsZ depletion would alter these localization patterns by the inhibition of FtsI with aztreonam. Again, the localization of DnaK-GFP to foci was dependent on growth at 42°C and was independent of the strain genotype or inhibition of the cell division pathway (90% of ftsZ84 cells and 87% of wild-type cells showed foci at 42°C versus 3% and 10%, respectively, at 30°C [Fig. 5B, D, F, and H ]), confirming the role of elevated temperature in the induction of DnaK-GFP localization to discrete foci. We next asked whether the sites to which DnaK-GFP localizes at elevated temperature overlap with those to which IcsA localizes, which we have previously demonstrated are at or near potential cell division sites ( 36 ). The simultaneous expression of DnaK-GFP (from pCA24N- dnaK ) with IcsA 507-620 -mCherry (from pBAD33- icsA 50 7 - 6 20 -mCherry) in wild-type MC4100 cells that had been filamented by the addition of aztreonam, demonstrated that the two proteins colocalize (Fig. 6A ), indicating that under these conditions, DnaK-GFP localizes at or near potential cell division sites. As expected, the localization of DnaK-GFP to these sites was independent of IcsA, since DnaK-GFP displayed an indistinguishable distribution in cells expressing mCherry instead of icsA 50 7 - 620 ::mCherry (93% ± 0.3% versus 93% ± 0.2% of cells, respectively, displayed foci at regular intervals [Fig. 6B ]). We next examined the distribution of native DnaK in wild-type cells at 42°C and 30°C, using immunofluorescence in cells that had been fixed and permeabilized (Fig. 7 ). For cells grown at 42°C, the signal from DnaK was present throughout the cytoplasm with clear enhancement at midcell and at the poles (Fig. 7D , left panel). A similar pattern was seen for cells grown at 30°C, although the signal was substantially weaker (Fig. 7A , left panel), consistent with the lower expression of DnaK at this temperature. For cells grown at 42°C, labeling for DnaK in cells that express both the DnaK-GFP fusion and native DnaK showed both the enhancement at midcell and bright polar foci (Fig. 7F , left panel), while the GFP signal was restricted to the pole (Fig. 7F , right panel). In contrast, for these same cells grown at 30°C, labeling for DnaK showed a pattern indistinguishable from that of the wild-type cells grown at the same temperature (Fig. 7C versus A, left panels), and the GFP signal was weak and diffuse (Fig. 7C , right panel). These results suggest that at 30°C, the distribution of DnaK-GFP reflects that of native DnaK, whereas at 42°C, DnaK-GFP localizes in a polar pattern that is substantially more focal than that of native DnaK, suggesting that at elevated temperatures, the localization of the GFP fusion protein does not fully reflect that of the native protein.

DISCUSSION.content: Our results indicate that in the absence of DnaK, the delivery of IcsA to the cell pole in the bacterial cytoplasm is defective. However, while our microscopy screen was designed to identify proteins that might serve as an FtsZ-independent receptor for IcsA at the pole and at potential cell division sites in the bacterial cytoplasm, DnaK itself is not such a receptor, and we did not identify one in the study. Indeed, the localization of DnaK to the pole and at potential cell division sites was unrelated to the premise of the screen. Nevertheless, DnaK has a critical role in the targeting and secretion not only of IcsA but also of SepA, anotherShigellaautotransporter.

DnaK functions as a chaperone and unfoldase in the bacterial cytoplasm, assisting the proper folding of substrate proteins and disaggregating proteins that have misfolded (49,65,76). On the basis of these observations, we postulate that the findings presented in this paper are most consistent with a role of DnaK in maintaining IcsA and SepA in a conformation that is competent for localization to the pole. We further postulate that IcsA and SepA localization to the pole per se occurs by a DnaK-independent mechanism, rather than DnaK being directly involved in the localization of IcsA to the pole. Our data indicate that the localization of DnaK in foci at the pole is not a prerequisite for the localization of IcsA to the pole. IcsA at the pole is normally rapidly secreted across the cytoplasmic membrane (9). The IcsA-GFP used in the present study was not competent for secretion; thus, the presence of polar foci of IcsA-GFP likely represents protein that has been delivered to the pole as a result of the proper chaperoning by DnaK but is no longer bound to DnaK. Taken together, our findings suggest a role for DnaK in the chaperoning of IcsA and SepA, and perhaps of autotransporter proteins more generally, in the bacterial cytoplasm.

Large multidomain proteins fold less efficiently and are more likely to require DnaK for folding than single-domain proteins, since the presence of multiple domains leads to the formation of partially folded intermediates that may contain misfolded states, which tend to aggregate (reviewed in references29,49, and76). Autotransporters are large proteins that contain at least three domains—an unusually long signal peptide, a large passenger domain, and a carboxy-terminal β-barrel domain. Autotransporters are secreted across the cytoplasmic membrane via the Sec apparatus (9,64). Then, in a coordinated fashion, the carboxy-terminal β-barrel is inserted into the outer membrane, and the passenger domain is translocated to the bacterial surface, although the mechanism by which these processes occur is poorly understood. At the bacterial surface, the passenger domains of all autotransporters for which the structures have been solved adopt a long, right-handed parallel β-helical conformation (20,22,37,52).

Proteins that are secreted via the Sec apparatus pass through the secretion channel in an unfolded conformation. While it is clear that the passenger domains of at least some autotransporters are at least partially folded in the periplasm (10,32), it is unclear whether this domain or any other domain is folded in the cytoplasm prior to secretion, in which case it would need to be unfolded prior to secretion across the cytoplasmic membrane. It seems likely that the role of DnaK is at a minimum to prevent the formation of partially folded intermediates of autotransporters that would be predisposed to aggregation, and possibly to also maintain autotransporters in a conformation that allows secretion through the Sec translocon and/or recognition of the positional information that directs the protein to the bacterial pole. Here, we found that in the absence of DnaK, IcsA-GFP formed irregularly shaped foci that distributed irregularly throughout the cytoplasm, a pattern highly suggestive of protein mistargeting and the formation of aggregates. Given the known functions of DnaK, it seems likely that these aggregates were due to the misfolding of unchaperoned IcsA. It is additionally possible that the loss of recognition of proper positional information that results from IcsA misfolding also contributes to the formation of aggregates.

Whereas DnaK displayed a diffuse distribution in theftsZ84strain at 30°C, most of the priority proteins identified in the screen as displaying foci at regular intervals along the lengths offtsZ84filaments at 42°C displayed polar foci in single cells of theftsZ84strain at 30°C (Table2). This suggests that while DnaK-GFP is dramatically influenced by temperature, most of the other GFP-tagged screen priority proteins are not, instead displaying similar distributions at 30°C and 42°C. We speculate that the redistribution of DnaK-GFP into foci at elevated temperatures is due in part to the increased interaction of the chaperone with substrate proteins. With heat shock, the pool of proteins that are in a nonnative state in the cytoplasm increases significantly (5,25,61,63,75). In heat-shocked cells, a YFP fusion to the small heat-shock protein IbpA (inclusion body protein A) colocalizes with inclusion bodies, and the IbpA-inclusion body complexes localize to cell poles, at midcell, and, in filaments, to potential cell division sites (44). Thus, the reported distribution of inclusion bodies and the IbpA chaperone mimics almost exactly the distribution we observed for DnaK-GFP, strongly suggesting that the redistribution of DnaK-GFP to these sites at elevated temperatures may be due in part to its interaction with nonnative or aggregated proteins at these sites.

Most of the proteins identified in our screen, when tagged with GFP, localize to cell poles and potential cell division sites independent of functional FtsZ and cell length (Table2). To our knowledge, this study represents the first genome-scale analysis of proteins that localize to the pole independent of FtsZ. Our analysis identified 92E. coliproteins, representing approximately 2% of the proteome, that display this localization pattern. Additional proteins may localize to cell poles in a manner dependent on FtsZ, but these would not have been identified in our screen. Among the proteins we identified, only the distribution of AmiC, an amidase that functions in cell division, has been previously determined. AmiC is localized to the septal ring during cell division and displays polar accumulation combined with a diffuse periplasmic signal at other times during the cell cycle (7), which reconciles with our finding that it localized to potential cell division sites in filaments lacking functional FtsZ.

While the localization of many of the GFP-tagged proteins identified in our screen likely recapitulates the distribution of the native protein, the localization observed for others likely does not, rather representing an artifact of the expression conditions or of the tagged construct itself. The distribution of a protein is influenced by the context of its synthesis, including whether it is stoichiometrically synthesized with interacting proteins (45). Protein aggregates frequently localize to cell poles (44), and the addition of a GFP tag to some of these proteins or the level of protein synthesis used in our analyses might induce nonnative folding, leading to aggregation. Moreover, certain of the “y” genes may be pseudogenes that are not normally expressed. An additional analysis of the native derivatives of this group of proteins will be necessary to determine which are truly localized to the poles.
